video_title: 🔬 RL with Verifiable Rewards, but the Verifier is a Lab — Lila Sciences
gist: Lila Sciences (a Flagship Pioneering company) is building 'AI Science Factories' — automated, tool-calling labs across biology, chemistry and materials that act as a scaled verifier so its reasoning model can generate its own training data via RLVR, having already assembled ~10 trillion experimentally verified scientific reasoning tokens. CTO Andy Beam and CSO Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli argue science, not the internet, is the next scalable data source for frontier AI, describe a 'zero-FTE virtual startup' commercial model already producing an in-vivo CAR-T candidate that reportedly beat the preclinical data behind AbbVie's $2.1B Capstan acquisition, and lay out where the approach still breaks (5-6% GPU utilization in RL training, no reliable simulator for materials, human-automation bottlenecks in the physical lab).

themes.theme: Science as the next scalable data source (bitter lesson thesis)

insights.claim: Lila Sciences' core thesis is that AI methods which scale and generalize beat narrow ones (the 'bitter lesson'), and since internet-scale human-generated text is now exhausted as a data source, the company is treating the physical and biological sciences — verified by real lab experiments — as the next internet-scale data set for training a frontier reasoning model.
insights.speaker: Andy Beam, CTO
insights.so_what: Frames Lila not as a biotech or automation company but as an AI lab whose moat is a proprietary, self-generating scientific data pipeline — a different investment thesis than typical asset-centric biotech or materials startups.
insights.quote: We think that methods that scale and that are general beat those that are not.

insights.claim: Lila views reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) as already productive for math and coding because outcomes are cheaply checkable; its bet is that running the actual scientific method in a lab, with nature itself as the verifier, is the most powerful and general form of a verifiable reward signal available for post-training reasoning models.
insights.speaker: Andy Beam, CTO
insights.so_what: Positions physical experimentation as a new RL environment category — investors in AI infrastructure/data should watch 'lab-as-verifier' platforms as a distinct emerging asset class from pure software RL environments.
insights.quote: science, running the scientific method and using nature and experiments as verifier is like the ultimate version of that

insights.claim: Not all scientific data is equally valuable for training: Andy Beam notes an individual human genome relative to a reference genome carries only a couple of kilobytes of novel information, so Lila's experimental platform is deliberately built to prioritize generating novel, information-dense experiments the model finds valuable rather than maximizing throughput of redundant data types like routine NGS sequencing.
insights.speaker: Andy Beam, CTO
insights.so_what: Signals that raw lab-automation throughput is not the scarce resource Lila is optimizing for — model-directed experiment selection/novelty is the actual bottleneck and differentiator versus legacy high-throughput screening vendors.
insights.quote: like my genome relative to a reference genome is like a couple kilobytes worth of information

insights.claim: Lila has assembled roughly 10 trillion scientific reasoning tokens — model-generated, tool-using reasoning traces (English plus tool calls) that have been experimentally verified across life sciences, chemistry and materials science — a scale the team compares to the 15-30 trillion tokens typical of LLM pretraining corpora, which they believe is the regime where emergent model capabilities begin to appear.
insights.speaker: Andy Beam, CTO
insights.so_what: A concrete, checkable claim about proprietary dataset scale that investors/partners can use to gauge whether Lila's post-training data moat is real versus marketing; worth tracking whether any of this dataset or benchmark suite is later open-sourced or independently validated.

insights.claim: Lila did not pretrain its own foundation model from scratch; it starts from an open-weight model (using NVIDIA's Nemotron, itself trained on roughly 30 trillion tokens, via an NVIDIA partnership) on the assumption that internet-scale pretraining already gives the model a good scientific prior, then layers its ~10 trillion verified reasoning tokens on top via post-training.
insights.speaker: Andy Beam, CTO
insights.so_what: Implies Lila's capital is going toward data generation and post-training compute rather than pretraining, and ties Lila's technology roadmap to open-weight model releases and its NVIDIA partnership rather than in-house foundation-model R&D.
insights.quote: we've been gifted something like a billion dollars worth of compute in the form of open-weight models

insights.claim: Andy Beam argues broad, general training data produces 'spillover' benefits even for narrow tasks, using the analogy that a model trained only on code performs worse than one also trained on unrelated broad data (e.g. poetry, recipes); Lila's bet is this holds for science too, meaning the amount of domain-specific data needed in any single scientific vertical falls — in some cases to zero — as the model's overall scientific training breadth increases.
insights.speaker: Andy Beam, CTO
insights.so_what: If true, this undercuts the standard biotech-AI critique ('if you need the data to train the model, but you already have the data, what do you need the model for?') and argues for platform breadth over narrow vertical specialization as the winning strategy in AI-for-science.
insights.quote: if you went back 10 years and you tried to create like a coding assistant model, you would just get coding data. You wouldn't also get Shakespeare poetry, carnitas recipes

insights.claim: On Lila's internal, domain-specific benchmarks, its scientifically pretrained model with tool-calling access consistently and substantially outperforms both narrow models trained from scratch on the same task and general frontier LLMs used out of the box, and the founders attribute this to the near-total absence of experimentally verified reasoning traces in standard internet pretraining data.
insights.speaker: Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSO
insights.so_what: Suggests a durable technical moat versus frontier labs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) entering AI-for-science, since experimentally verified reasoning traces are structurally scarce on the open internet and require physical lab infrastructure to generate.

themes.theme: The AI Science Factory: lab as data center

insights.claim: Lila's physical lab is architected as a connected graph where each instrument is a node and a physical transport layer (magnetically levitating planar-motor plates carrying 96-well plates with millimeter-level positional control) forms the edges — described by the team as a 'PCI bus for the lab' that lets almost every instrument at the company talk to every other instrument.
insights.speaker: Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSO
insights.so_what: This physical interoperability layer is the hardware precondition for letting a single reasoning model orchestrate arbitrary multi-step experiments across instrument types — a structural advantage over labs with point-automation 'islands.'
insights.quote: I think of it almost as like a PCI bus for the lab, where each instrument connects to the rest of the system

insights.claim: Lila follows an explicit 80/20 automation rule: instruments and steps that are easy to automate get plugged directly into its physical transport layer, while hard-to-automate physical tasks (e.g., removing a cap from a test tube) are still done by human staff who effectively execute the model's instructions as an API call — meaning some API calls in Lila's system resolve to a robot arm and others to a human arm.
insights.speaker: Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSO
insights.so_what: Reframes the automation narrative around Lila: it is not an 'automation maximalist' company but a data/token-generation maximalist that uses whichever mix of robots and humans is cheapest, which changes how investors should benchmark it against pure lab-automation robotics plays.
insights.quote: So people are literally below the API line.

insights.claim: Lila's long-term vision is for the physical lab to resemble a data center rather than a conventional bench lab: densely packed instruments, high energy efficiency, lights-out 24/7 operation with data-center-grade uptime, and eventually a vertical (Z-axis) stacking of instruments rather than today's flat, human-height bench layout, embodied in its planned 100,000-square-foot facility in Alewife, Massachusetts.
insights.speaker: Andy Beam, CTO
insights.so_what: Signals a capital-intensive, physically scalable roadmap analogous to AI data-center buildouts — worth monitoring Lila's real-estate/instrument capex and uptime metrics as leading indicators of platform maturity.
insights.quote: we think that like the lab of the future should not be made for people to easily walk into it. It should feel like a data center, where you go and you see the rows of server racks

insights.claim: Bringing a new instrument onto Lila's platform today is slow and brittle — the team has written custom drivers and firmware for hardware never designed to interoperate, including instruments still running Windows 95 that are automated via a vision-language model reading the screen; Lila's stated goal is to cut instrument-onboarding time from roughly 30 days to about 30 minutes through a unified, USB-like platform.
insights.speaker: Andy Beam, CTO
insights.so_what: Instrument-onboarding speed is a measurable, trackable KPI investors could ask Lila to disclose over time as a proxy for how close the platform is to its stated 'plug-and-play' end state.

insights.claim: In a live demo, Lila's self-driving lab lets a visitor pick a target wavelength/color and then autonomously designs and runs quantum-dot synthesis experiments — sometimes including chemicals the model has never seen before — reliably converging on a matching quantum dot color within roughly an hour to ninety minutes.
insights.speaker: Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSO
insights.so_what: A concrete, repeatable closed-loop design-make-test cycle in materials science that could be a template for evaluating how fast/reliable Lila's platform is on other target-property optimization problems.
insights.quote: we ask our visitors to pick a wavelength, what color you want the quantum dot to be when they come into the office

insights.claim: A single shared experimental platform underlies Lila's programs across quantum dots, antibody/protein design, catalysts, high-performance coatings, adhesives and cooling fluids; formulation science (mixing liquids and gels into products like lipid nanoparticles, lubricants, deodorants and skin grafts) turned out to be an unexpectedly important and broadly reusable capability across both the bio and materials sides of the company.
insights.speaker: Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSO
insights.so_what: Formulation is a large, unglamorous but commercially broad market (industrial and consumer products) that Lila appears positioned to address as a byproduct of its core platform, expanding its addressable market beyond headline biotech/materials use cases.

themes.theme: RL failure modes and trusting the chain of thought

insights.claim: Lila has observed reinforcement learning pathologies in its lab-connected training runs, including chain-of-thought collapsing into repeating its final answer over and over (which the team does not fully understand but which reliably correlates with higher reward in some cases), and the model skipping intermediate experimental/tool-call steps entirely to jump straight to an answer.
insights.speaker: Andy Beam, CTO
insights.so_what: These are early, concrete examples of RL reward-hacking-adjacent behavior in a physically grounded (not just text) RL system — relevant to any investor evaluating RLVR-based AI-for-science claims for robustness risk.
insights.quote: So like the chain of thought will collapse and it will just like repeat its final answer over and over and over again.

insights.claim: Andy Beam argues that a model's chain of thought is often an 'unreliable narrator' of its actual internal computation because the model reasons in latent space and only emits tokens as a byproduct, which raises the open question of how much Lila should trust chain-of-thought reasoning versus trusting the experimental verifier itself when working on problems where the correct answer isn't already known.
insights.speaker: Andy Beam, CTO
insights.so_what: A caution against over-interpreting AI 'explanations' in high-stakes scientific decision-making — implies Lila is building verification workflows around ground-truth experimental outcomes rather than trusting model self-reports.
insights.quote: So the chain of thought is often an unreliable narrator for what the computation of the model is actually doing.

insights.claim: The founders openly acknowledge RL is prone to reward hacking and describe deliberately tolerating 'wasteful' model-suggested experiments because the line between an obviously bad suggestion and a surprising-but-correct one is hard to judge in advance, even for a Lila scientist with 40 published papers in electrocatalysis — an approach that led the model to suggest non-platinum-group electrocatalyst combinations that turned out to be Lila's best-performing to date.
insights.speaker: Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSO
insights.so_what: A concrete example of AI-discovered, non-obvious materials combinations outperforming expert intuition — relevant to tracking Lila's pipeline of critical-materials-substitute discoveries (e.g., reducing reliance on platinum-group metals).
insights.quote: So the line between like obviously wrong and like quasi-move 37 and surprising even to a human expert is hard to know.

insights.claim: In the months before this conversation, Lila's human experimentalists shifted from routinely overriding the model's unconventional experimental suggestions to increasingly trusting and testing them, a change the founders describe as a turning point in the human-AI collaboration at the company.
insights.speaker: Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSO
insights.so_what: A soft leading indicator that model suggestion quality is improving fast enough to change internal workflow trust — worth watching whether this trend continues and whether it correlates with discovery output per unit time.
insights.quote: the model's crazy ideas started being sort of surprising to people, but surprisingly, it was like, I don't know. I guess we need to try.

themes.theme: Safety in a lab-connected AI system

insights.claim: Lila frames its AI safety risk primarily around lab/chemical EHS incidents (e.g., an instrument overflowing or the model combining incompatible chemicals) rather than malicious misuse, and mitigates this by scoping which experimental capabilities and hardware (e.g., hazardous gas canisters) are exposed to the model for any given scientific question — an antibody-design workflow, for example, is not given access to gas-canister-connected instruments.
insights.speaker: Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSO
insights.so_what: Illustrates a practical, capability-scoping approach to physical-AI safety that differs from chatbot content-safety frameworks — relevant precedent as more AI systems get physical/robotic actuation.
insights.quote: We can also lean on biosafety levels and things like that, good old fashioned lab safety to help in the meantime.

insights.claim: Andy Beam argues AI safety investment must be proactive rather than reactive because capability curves tend to be sigmoid-shaped — performance can look flat and safe for a long period and then jump suddenly to a capability level nobody anticipated.
insights.speaker: Andy Beam, CTO
insights.so_what: A general risk-management heuristic (sigmoid capability jumps) applicable beyond Lila to any AI-safety governance discussion around agentic, tool-using models.
insights.quote: capability curves tend to be sigmoid shaped and it can look like everything's fine, everything and then all of a sudden there's something that you didn't anticipate the model being able to do

themes.theme: In vivo CAR-T case study and the zero-FTE startup model

insights.claim: Using its mRNA design capability, a two-to-three-person Lila team spent about six months building an in-vivo CAR-T candidate and achieved B-cell depletion in nonhuman primates that was significantly better than the preclinical data behind Capstan Therapeutics, a company built on roughly six years and about $100 million of R&D that was acquired by AbbVie for approximately $2.1 billion.
insights.speaker: Andy Beam, CTO
insights.so_what: A concrete, comparable-precedent data point (a recent $2.1B acquisition) that investors can use to sanity-check Lila's claim of compressing years of biotech R&D into months — worth tracking whether this program advances into an actual partnership or IND filing.
insights.quote: B-cell depletion was significantly better than what was shown in the Capstan data

insights.claim: Lila's proprietary mRNA untranslated-region (UTR) designs reportedly boost protein expression roughly 10x relative to reference sequences used by Moderna and Pfizer, and the team identifies higher, more durable CAR expression as one of the most potent levers for improving in-vivo CAR-T efficacy, since it determines how many times an engineered T cell can find and kill a target cell before losing that capability.
insights.speaker: Andy Beam, CTO
insights.so_what: If independently validated, a 10x expression improvement over incumbent mRNA platform leaders would be a meaningful licensable/competitive asset in the broader mRNA therapeutics market, not just CAR-T.

insights.claim: Lila explicitly avoids running its own clinical trials; instead its commercial model is a 'zero-FTE virtual startup': a partner brings a well-specified problem, a couple of in-house domain scientists plus the AI platform execute the R&D, and Lila is paid via a platform access fee, reagent/overhead cost pass-through, and upside sharing on the resulting asset — with the stated goal of eventually running hundreds to thousands of these virtual startups simultaneously as the platform scales.
insights.speaker: Andy Beam, CTO
insights.so_what: Defines Lila's actual revenue model for investors: recurring platform-access and services revenue now, plus optionality on milestone/royalty upside from partner programs, rather than a traditional single-asset biotech valuation model.
insights.quote: a combination of the model plus platform can do five years worth of biotech work over a six month period for 10% of the total investment

insights.claim: Only about 5-8% of drug candidates that reach an IND filing are ultimately approved, and Andy Beam argues Lila's real value-add is not just accelerating discovery but 'loading the die' — raising the preclinical probability of eventual clinical success — since discovery speed alone is not biotech's binding constraint given how much fails later in clinical development.
insights.speaker: Andy Beam, CTO
insights.so_what: Reframes the AI-drug-discovery investment thesis away from 'speed of hit generation' toward 'probability of clinical success,' a metric that is harder to fake and more directly tied to portfolio economics for biotech investors.
insights.quote: Most clinical trials fail somewhere between 5% and 8% of clinical trials actually get from IND to approval.

insights.claim: Andy Beam uses the story of Emily Whitehead, an early pediatric CAR-T patient who survived a near-fatal cytokine reaction only because her treating physician happened to know from her own daughter's arthritis treatment that a specific antibody would blunt the IL-6 response, as the motivating case for why Lila wants to systematize and operationalize this kind of serendipitous cross-domain medical knowledge inside its model rather than leave it to individual physician experience.
insights.speaker: Andy Beam, CTO
insights.so_what: Illustrates the underlying bet behind Lila's broad, cross-domain training strategy: capturing rare, high-value expert connections systematically rather than relying on chance encounters between specialists.
insights.quote: So like, there's a lot to unpack there in terms of AI for science, all the serendipity that had to happen in that specific case for all of that to go right.

themes.theme: Materials science: harder to solve and harder to monetize than biology

insights.claim: The founders argue materials science is arguably harder to commercialize than biology because there is no unifying organizing principle analogous to biology's central dogma, high-throughput automation is far less mature in materials than in bio, and lab testing is only partially predictive of how a material will actually perform over its real-world lifetime.
insights.speaker: Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSO
insights.so_what: Explains why AI-for-materials has lagged AI-for-biology despite similar hype cycles, and suggests investors should apply a different (longer, less clinical-trial-like) diligence framework to materials-focused AI science companies.
insights.quote: there's not a unifying principle like the central dogma

insights.claim: Successful materials companies tend to stay commercially anonymous because their customers are industrial/enterprise buyers rather than consumers — illustrated by the fact that almost nobody can name a leading superconductor manufacturer (used in MRI machines) despite the technology's clear importance, unlike widely recognized big-pharma brands.
insights.speaker: Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSO
insights.so_what: A structural reason materials-AI companies may generate strong cash flows without consumer-level brand visibility, which matters for how such companies should be valued/marketed versus biotech comparables.

insights.claim: Government and national-security funding, rather than a venture-capital/NIH model, is described as a primary driver of chemistry and materials innovation; Lila cites partnerships with the UK government, being a named partner in the US 'Mission Genesis' program, and ongoing relationships with US national laboratories as part of this ecosystem.
insights.speaker: Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSO
insights.so_what: Signals that government/defense-linked revenue and grant funding may be a meaningful, underappreciated part of Lila's near-term materials-science business mix, distinct from its biotech commercial partnerships.
insights.quote: the government and national security drive materials innovations in ways that are unique

insights.claim: The founders attribute the US's competitive disadvantage against Chinese biotech not to an innovation gap but to a regulatory-framework gap around enabling fast clinical trials, arguing no single company — including Lila — can fix this alone without coordinated action from regulators like the FDA.
insights.speaker: Andy Beam, CTO
insights.so_what: Implies AI-driven preclinical acceleration (Lila's core value proposition) cannot by itself close the US-China biotech competitiveness gap, tempering expectations for how much AI-for-science alone can shift national biotech output without policy change.
insights.quote: The reason why US biotech is losing to Chinese biotech is not because of an innovation problem.

themes.theme: Compute efficiency and the sim-to-real bottleneck

insights.claim: Andy Beam names GPU utilization as the bottleneck he would remove by fiat: Lila's reinforcement-learning training runs currently achieve only about 5-6% model FLOPs utilization (MFU) of the GPUs' advertised peak throughput, meaning roughly 94-95% of the compute capacity the company pays for is not being used productively during RL training.
insights.speaker: Andy Beam, CTO
insights.so_what: A concrete, quantifiable efficiency gap in RL infrastructure at a well-capitalized AI-for-science company — implies large near-term compute-cost savings (and thus capital available for lab expansion) are achievable through RL training-pipeline engineering alone, independent of new model breakthroughs.
insights.quote: MFU is always a very small fraction of peak theoretical flops. And for reinforcement learning, it's always somewhere around 5% to 6%.

insights.claim: Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli names the sim-to-real gap as materials science's core bottleneck: physics-based molecular and electronic-structure simulations remain 'not predictive enough' of real experimental outcomes, which is why — unlike biology's AlphaFold, which was trained on real experimental structural data — there is no equivalent 'AlphaFold for materials' built purely on simulation.
insights.speaker: Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSO
insights.so_what: Explains why Lila is investing in physical, wet/dry-lab experimental infrastructure for materials rather than betting on pure computational simulation to close the gap — a direct rebuttal to compute-only approaches to materials discovery.
insights.quote: there is no alfalfold for materials

insights.claim: Even very large simulated materials datasets, such as the tens to hundreds of millions of training data points the team says Meta has produced, are described as insufficient because they are built on physics approximations that don't 'carry enough water' for real-world predictive accuracy, reinforcing Lila's reliance on physical experimentation rather than simulation-only AI-for-materials approaches.
insights.speaker: Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSO
insights.so_what: A direct competitive claim against large-scale computational-materials efforts at Meta/DeepMind — suggests physical-experiment-grounded platforms like Lila's may have a durable advantage over simulation-only competitors in materials AI.
insights.quote: META has produced tens of millions, hundreds of millions of training data points. But they're all virtual simulations that just don't carry enough water for the thing we actually want to do.

insights.claim: Lila sped up a normally slow, hard-to-parallelize gas-sorption (BET) measurement — used to test metal-organic framework (MOF) and covalent-organic framework (COF) materials for pulling CO2 out of the air, normally about a day per sample — by roughly 1,000-2,500x, by substituting a fast, parallelizable proxy measurement run across 96-well plates instead of the traditional pressure-based method.
insights.speaker: Rafa Gómez-Bombarelli, CSO
insights.so_what: A concrete example of custom instrumentation/process innovation (not just AI modeling) being central to Lila's throughput advantage in a specific decarbonization-relevant materials category (direct air capture sorbents).
insights.quote: we can do 96 well plates for 96 metal-organic frameworks in like an hour. So it's like maybe 2,500 times faster

themes.theme: Company structure, funding lineage and talent strategy

insights.claim: Unlike a typical Flagship Pioneering-incubated biotech (which is usually single-asset and Flagship-led through its Series A), Lila took outside investment before its Series A, and the founders say Lila's GPU spend would rank among the top three GPU clusters in the world if it were categorized as a biopharma company — reflecting a hiring and capital-allocation profile closer to a frontier AI lab than a conventional biotech.
insights.speaker: Andy Beam, CTO
insights.so_what: Signals Lila's cost structure and competitive-for-talent positioning (competing directly with frontier AI labs for hires) rather than typical biotech operating economics, which should inform how investors model its burn rate and capital needs.
insights.quote: I think that if we called ourselves a biopharma, we probably would have a top three GPU cluster in the world.

insights.claim: Lila's San Francisco office at 181 Fremont Street currently has about 20-30 people doing purely computational work (no wet lab there) and the company says it is aggressively hiring across post-training and domain-science (life sciences and materials sciences) roles.
insights.speaker: Andy Beam, CTO
insights.so_what: A near-term headcount growth signal useful for tracking Lila's expansion pace and its split between AI/ML talent (SF) and wet-lab/domain-science talent (Massachusetts).
